observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory of all properties of the municipality, submit as soon as possible the RPCI and RPCPPE and classify the Inventory Report according to their respective account code classification.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant always record the fines and penalties collected on unpaid real property taxes and special education taxes to the appropriate account in conformity with the International Public Sector Accounting Standards Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units in order to present fairly the financial statements at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that discounts for payments in advance be recognized and accounted as Discount on RPT/SET as required under the International Public Sector Accounting Standards Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units in order to present fairly the financial statements of the Municipality at year-end.

The Audit Team believes that even though the entries were provided by the ETRACT System, the Municipal Accountant should book up the discount which can be computed and recorded manually using the Report of Collections.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting entries to present fairly the financial statements at year-end.

The Audit Team believes that although the funds were given as financial assistance to Gunglos and WID, it should still be recorded as Loans Receivable since it will be collected by the Municipal Treasurer and will again be rolled over. Also, the proper recording of the funds to Loans Receivable will help the Municipal Agriculture Office in the monitoring of the funds.

We recommended that Management exhaust all possible remedies to collect the long overdue accounts. In the event that the exhaustion of available remedies for the collection of these dormant receivables, the Municipality should take all the necessary procedures to facilitate the writing-off as provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly follow the proper accounting entries in recording wages of job orders and contracts of service as prescribed in the International Public Sector Accounting Standards Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units.

The Audit Team believes that these expenses should be classified on its proper expense class so that the Municipal Accountant will be able to record it correctly.

We recommended that payment of medical benefits of municipal officials and employees be made in conformity with Section 3 of Administrative Order No. 402, CSC Memorandum Circular No. 33, s. 1997 dated December 22, 1997 and Administrative Code of the Municipality in order to avoid disallowance in audit.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the prohibition on double compensation as provided in Section 8 of Article 9(B) of the 1987 Constitution in order to prevent wastage of government funds and disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9679 in observing the maximum limit of PAG-IBIG contribution government share at Php100.00 per month per official/employee to avoid overpayment of premiums.
We recommended that Management exercise prudence in the disbursements of government funds in order to safeguard against loss or wastage.

We recommended that Management require the abovementioned documents to support the petty cash transaction of Php1,000.00 and above in order to ascertain the validity and regularity of petty cash transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant determine the correct balances of the SEF and Trust Fund and the excess fund should be transferred to the bank account maintained under the General Fund in order to maintain separate depository accounts for each fund in compliance with Section 310 of RA 7160 otherwise known as the Local Government Code of 1991. Further, we recommended that a cashbook be maintained by the Municipal Treasurer on DBP Account No. 0545-039-07Y-530.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should be guided by Section 31, Chapter 2 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for LGUs, Vol. II in order to have a basis for recording in the appropriate journals.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be guided as required by the aforementioned existing rules and regulations under the IPSAS.

We recommended that the practice of using funds other than those specifically provided in the budget be stopped, otherwise disallowed in audit. Further we recommended that management augment exhausted budgets from savings in other items within the same expense class expenditures.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly implement the procedures in the issuances and monitoring of official receipts as prescribed in Section 59 of Local Treasury Operations Manual of the Bureau of Local Government Finance dated October 1, 2007.

We recommended that Management strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 96-010 on the proper accomplishment of POs and include brand names together with the specifications of the items quoted by the supplier to serve as reference during deliveries and inspection and acceptance by the management.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations in order to effect the propriety, validity and legality of the transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly withhold income taxes from the compensation of various resources speakers and lecturers in accordance with Section 2.79 of Revenue Regulation (RR) 2-98 as amended by Section 7 of RR No. 11-2018.
We recommended that Management stop the practice of allowing employee claims for registration and similar fees from government funds not covered by NBC 563 dated April 26, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the schools and barangays who are recipients of the monetary awards to issue official receipts in order that said monetary awards be accounted for as government funds in the books of the barangays and be subjected to appropriations ordinance and the usual accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

We recommended that management see to it that subsequent claims of BAC honoraria should be based on the rates prescribed by DBM Budget Circular No. 2005-5A and that the reference for the actual claim of such honoraria are the list of successfully completed projects together with the Notice of Awards of each project. Further, succeeding claims in excess of the allowable BAC honorarium will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management undertake the following:
Stop the practice of allowing these job order/contracts of service/non-employees to attend trainings and seminars and claim the corresponding travelling allowances and per diems;
Grant only the actual fare from the worker’s official station to the place of destination and vice versa whenever they are directed to perform tasks outside their official station; and
Allow only permanent or casual employee to act as official driver whenever there is a need to transport municipal employee/s to attend trainings, seminars and/or perform other tasks.

Further we recommended that succeeding claims of municipal workers hired under contract of service, job order contracts and non-employees on travelling allowances, per diems and incidental expenses in attending trainings/seminars and meetings and in performing various tasks outside official station will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review and determine the proper accounts to which these various liabilities be recorded and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to fairly present the financial statements of the municipality at year end.

Further, we recommended that management issue a demand letter to the concerned officials or employees who failed to liquidate their cash advances within the required time frame or at year-end.

We recommended that the concerned employee immediately liquidate his previous cash advance if the purpose for which is was intended has already been completed or refund to the collecting officer when it is no longer needed or has not been used before claiming his unpaid reimbursements. Also, we recommended the Municipal Accountant to always see to it that unliquidated cash advances shall not be offset with the claims of the accountable officers.

We recommended that the management strictly enforce that only duly appointed and designated disbursing officer shall perform disbursing function and cash advance shall be used solely for the specific legal purpose for which it was granted in order to avoid possible misuse of government funds.

We recommended that a liquidation report be prepared and used in upon liquidating the cash advances granted employee and officials. Further, refrain from using disbursement voucher in reporting the liquidation of cash advances in order to properly monitor it.

We recommended that confidential expenses made by officials be directly submitted to the COA Chairperson for review and audit as required under the general guidelines of the Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015 re: Guidelines on the Entitlement, Release, Use, Reporting and Audit of Confidential and/or Intelligence Funds.

We recommended that management purchase common-use supplies from the PS-DBM at least quarterly in order to avail the most advantageous price. We recommended that management adhere to existing guidelines in the computation of ABC to avoid disallowance in the audit. Also, we recommended that the amount of Php788,527.85 be deducted from the next billing or final payment.

We recommended that management (a) require the concerned municipal employees to immediately refund the PIB received covering the CY 2017 and any subsequent disbursement, if any, for the same employee benefit; (b) discontinue appropriating funds for the payment of PIB for the succeeding years to avoid audit disallowances; and (c) grant Step Increment/s Due to Meritorious Performance instead of PIB to municipal employees who exemplify outstanding performances provided the provisions of CSC-DBM Joint Circular 1-2012 are met.

We recommended the immediate submission of the employee’s individual appraisal or evaluation reports or employee’s contribution in compliance to the above-mentioned provisions of law and to avoid suspension in audit.

We recommended that management:
Require the refund from the concerned casual and job order employees the total amount of Php242,000.00.
Stop the grant of financial assistance to job order employees without legal basis.

We recommended that management formulate a written policy guidelines in assessing the necessity of hiring contractual and job order employees taking into consideration the reasonable number need by the different offices in order to avoid excessive workforce, idle manhours and work duplication.

We have recommended that the municipal accountant make the necessary adjustments on the erroneous recording on the transfer of fund to the Second Party to present fairly the financial statements of the agency as of December 31, 2016.

We have recommended to management that the LDRRM  Officer refrain from charging non-priority expenses in the LDRRMF and instead, assess the adequacy and availability of basic rescue and response equipment in the LDRRM Office and prioritize the procurement of equipment useful for disaster prevention, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.

We have further recommended that the LDRRM Officer revisit the above mentioned guidelines on the utilization of LDRRMF before charging certain expenditures against the said fund.

We have recommended that   management   implement the ecological solid waste management to ensure protection of public health and environment.  Also,   the purchase of lot for eco-waste be made for its speedy and proper implementation for the benefits of the public in general.

We have recommended that the municipal accountant should determine the total uncollected balances of the extended loans and debit this to loans receivables and credit to prior year’s adjustment. Likewise, the  municipality should discontinue the scheme of increasing the amount of loan granted to farmers yearly and focus on the collection of the same to avoid loss of government funds.
